questions.explanation: A list preserves order and allows duplicates, which is ideal for an ordered collection of file paths.
questions.question: In Python, which data structure is most appropriate for storing an ordered sequence of image file paths that may contain duplicates?

questions.explanation: WHERE filters individual rows prior to GROUP BY and aggregation; HAVING filters after aggregation.
questions.question: In SQL, which clause is used to filter rows BEFORE aggregation (e.g., before computing COUNT or AVG)?

questions.explanation: Convolution applies learnable kernels across the image to detect local features that are useful for recognition tasks.

options.A: Applies learnable filters to extract local patterns (e.g., edges, textures)
options.B: Sorts pixels by intensity to reduce noise
options.C: Converts RGB images to grayscale only
options.D: Randomly drops pixels to prevent overfitting

questions.question: In computer vision, what does a convolutional layer primarily do to an input image?

questions.explanation: Precision-Recall AUC focuses on performance for the positive class and is more informative than accuracy under class imbalance.
questions.question: Which metric is most appropriate for evaluating a binary classifier when the positive class (e.g., 'defect') is rare in a vision dataset?

questions.explanation: Using test-set statistics leaks information from the evaluation data into preprocessing, biasing results and inflating performance estimates.

options.A: It increases GPU memory usage during training
options.B: It causes label leakage into the features
options.C: It introduces data leakage, making evaluation overly optimistic
options.D: It prevents the model from converging

questions.question: You are training a vision model and want to standardize input images using per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the training set. Why should you avoid computing these statistics using the test set?

questions.explanation: In frequentist terms, 95% refers to long-run coverage: across repeated samples, 95% of such intervals contain the true parameter.

options.A: There is a 95% probability the true mean lies in this specific computed interval
options.B: If you repeated the sampling process many times, about 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true mean
options.C: 95% of individual latency measurements fall inside the interval
options.D: The model is correct 95% of the time

questions.question: In statistics, if you compute a 95% confidence interval for a model’s mean inference latency, what does '95%' most accurately mean?

questions.explanation: A group-based split prevents leakage of product-specific visual cues into validation and better estimates performance on unseen products.

options.A: Random train/validation split across all images
options.B: K-fold cross-validation with random shuffling of all images
options.C: Group-based split where all images of a product are kept in the same fold (e.g., GroupKFold)
options.D: Use the training set for validation to maximize data usage

questions.question: You have 1,000 labeled images from 50 different physical products (20 images per product). To best estimate generalization to NEW products, which validation strategy is most appropriate?

questions.explanation: Freezing requires disabling gradients for backbone parameters (requires_grad=False) and ensuring the optimizer updates only the head parameters.

options.B: Set requires_grad=False for backbone parameters and pass only head parameters to the optimizer
options.C: Wrap the forward pass in torch.no_grad() for the entire model during training
options.D: Increase the learning rate so the head learns faster than the backbone

questions.question: In PyTorch, you want to fine-tune a pretrained CNN by freezing the backbone and training only a new classification head. Which approach correctly prevents backbone weights from being updated?

questions.explanation: Keras mixed precision uses float16 where safe and float32 where needed; loss scaling mitigates gradient underflow and is standard practice.

options.A: Set the global policy to 'mixed_float16' and use a loss scaling optimizer (automatically handled in modern Keras)
options.B: Cast all model weights to float16 manually and disable BatchNorm layers
options.C: Convert the input pipeline to int8 and keep the model in float32
options.D: Use float64 for the loss to avoid underflow

questions.question: In TensorFlow/Keras, you are training a segmentation model and want to use mixed precision to speed up training on a modern GPU while keeping numerical stability. Which is the best practice?

questions.explanation: A useful digital twin must be calibrated to the real system and continuously updated/validated with real data to ensure sim-to-real transfer and trustworthy decisions.

options.A: A 3D simulation that looks realistic, even if it does not match camera intrinsics/extrinsics or lighting
options.B: A digital twin calibrated to real camera parameters, lighting, and process variability, with a feedback loop that updates the twin using real sensor data
options.C: A dataset of only real images, with no simulation, because digital twins cannot be used for ML
options.D: A simulation that generates unlimited synthetic images but never validates against real-world performance

questions.question: You are building a digital twin of a factory line to improve a vision-based defect detector. Which setup best supports reliable model improvement and deployment decisions?
